content.problemStatement:
We will now look at the classic problem of finding the longest increasing subsequence (LIS) in an array.

Consider an array $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$, where every $A_i$ is a positive integer. We want to find a subsequence (ie. a subset of the elements, which don't need to be contiguous, but should be in the same order as in the array), in which every element is at least as large as the previous element, and whoselength is as large as possible.

For example, if the array is $[5, 2, 4, 4, 8, 1, 10]$, the answer would be $5$, as the subsequence $[2, 4, 4, 8, 10]$ is increasing (ie $S_i \leq S_j$, for all $i \leq j$), and has a length of $5$, and there is no longer increasing subsequence.

Similar to the previous problems, when you start looking at a possible DP solution, the first choice of sub-problem is to select $DP[i]$ to be the answer for the first $i$ elements. But just like the previous problem, we run into the issue of not knowing what the last element of $DP[i-1]$ is. And so we won't be able to guarantee that the subsequence we build is actually increasing.

We know how to solve this issue though - change the DP definition to always include the last element!

$DP[i]$ is going to define the maximum length we can get from an increasing subsequence of the first $i$ elements, **but** with the extra constraint that the subsequence should end at $A_i$. 

What would the final answer be? It can't be $DP[N]$, as it need not necessarily end with $A_N$. So all we have to do is just take the maximum of all the DP values, since the actual optimal subsequence could end at any index. Great, this seems doable.

So now let's get back to the DP equation. What is $DP[i]$ now?

What are the options? $A_i$ has to definitely be selected. We don't have a choice in that anymore. The only choice is in what the previous element should be. We have $i-1$ choices for that. Some of them will be valid, and some won't. And we will only consider the valid options. $A_j$ can be previous element only if $A_j \leq A_i$ - that's our validity condition.

So, $DP[i] = \max_{j=1}^{j = i-1} \{1 + DP[j], \text{ if } A_j \leq A_i\}$.

Also, don't forget the other option, which is that there is no previous element, and $A_i$ is the whole subsequence on its own with a length of $1$. For this, we just initialize $DP[i] = 1$ before looping over the other $i-1$ options.

And Final Answer = $\max\{DP[1], DP[2], \ldots, DP[N]\}$.

This is an $O(N^2)$ DP solution.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
